content:
<scheduled-task name="lightfast-xcom-opportunities" file="/Users/jeevanpillay/.claude/scheduled-tasks/lightfast-xcom-opportunities/SKILL.md">
This is an automated run of a scheduled task. The user is not present to answer questions. For implementation details, execute autonomously without asking clarifying questions — make reasonable choices and note them in your output. "write" actions (e.g. MCP tools that send, post, create, update, or delete), only take them if the task file asks for that specific action. When in doubt, producing a report of what you found is the correct output.

Find 5–10 high-quality posts on x.com where Lightfast (@lightfastai) can engage to nurture distribution. Research only — never post or reply. Output is for human review.

## Mission

Lightfast = the operating layer for product development. Memory + cited reasoning + agent action across GitHub, Vercel, Sentry, Linear. We're building the kernel agents and people both run on.

We engage to **nurture, not pitch**. The best replies make the original poster look smarter, not us. If a reply could be ghostwritten by a SaaS marketing team, it's wrong.

## Tooling — Browserbase MCP (cloud, step-by-step)

The MCP launches with `--contextId $BROWSERBASE_XCOM_CONTEXT_ID --persist true`. Sessions start already logged into x.com. Six primitives: `start`, `end`, `navigate`, `act`, `observe`, `extract`.

Loop:
1. `mcp__browserbase__start` → session in persisted Context.
2. `mcp__browserbase__navigate` → home or search URL.
3. `mcp__browserbase__extract` with a Zod-style schema → pull `{ author_handle, post_text, post_url, like_count, reply_count, repost_count, posted_ago }` for visible posts.
4. For promising authors, `navigate` to their profile, `extract` follower count + bio.
5. Score, dedupe, repeat until you have 5–10 quality candidates.
6. `mcp__browserbase__end` — cookies auto-persist back.

Drive each call. Do not delegate to a black-box agent.

## Search seeds

Start with these. Add 2–3 of your own based on what's trending the day of run.

- `https://x.com/search?q=agent%20memory&f=live`
- `https://x.com/search?q=MCP%20server&f=live`
- `https://x.com/search?q=Claude%20Code&f=live`
- `https://x.com/search?q=Cursor%20agent&f=live`
- `https://x.com/search?q=context%20for%20agents&f=live`
- `https://x.com/search?q=Mem0%20OR%20Zep%20OR%20Letta&f=live` (competitor mentions)
- `https://x.com/search?q=LangChain%20memory&f=live`
- `https://x.com/search?q=agents%20in%20production&f=live`
- `https://x.com/search?q=dev%20tool%20sprawl&f=live`
- `https://x.com/search?q=incident%20root%20cause&f=live`
- Also check: `x.com/lightfastai` mentions tab; replies under @AnthropicAI / @cursor_ai / @claude_code MCP launch threads.

## Author quality bar

**Auto-include:**
- 500+ followers AND bio mentions: founder, CTO, eng, builds, ships, AI, agents, dev tools, infra, researcher
- Verified blue + technical content (skip influencer / general-business blue checks)
- Anyone @lightfastai already follows or who follows back

**Auto-skip:**
- <500 followers (unless bio is bullseye ICP — *founder of <agent infra startup>*, *eng at <known AI lab>*)
- Bio is generic ("entrepreneur", "investor", "AI enthusiast" with no specifics)
- Last 5 posts are all hashtag-stuffed promo
- Account created in last 90 days with thread-bot posting cadence
- Engagement looks bot-amplified: 100+ likes / 0 replies, identical reply patterns

## Post quality bar

**Engage:**
- Genuine question or working-out-loud thinking about agents/memory/infra/MCP
- Strong opinion grounded in shipped work (not airport-takeoff threads)
- Bug report, frustration, or unmet need in the dev-tool stack — Lightfast-shaped problem
- Replies on launch / changelog threads from Anthropic, Cursor, Vercel, Linear, Sentry — high-context, builders are watching

**Skip:**
- Pure promotion / launch announcement of an MCP-aggregator / vibes product
- Politics, drama, hot-takes detached from work
- Posts older than 6 hours unless mega-thread (replies feel late after that window)
- Anything that already has 50+ replies — your reply gets buried

## Reply rubric — Lightfast voice

**Voice:** technical, curious, lowercase ok, dry. Never starts with *"great point"* / *"this"* / *"so true"*. Never pitches Lightfast unprompted. Never asks a bait question to fish for engagement. 1–10 words, hard cap.

**Good replies (engage):**
- *"persistent state across runs is the unlock"* (peer founder shipped a primitive)
- *"goal-fit signals beat recency every time"* (someone working out memory ranking)
- *"citations on every memory writeback helps"* (someone raising memory-poisoning)
- *"same — cold tool calls especially"* (someone complaining about Claude Code latency)

**Bad replies (would not ship):**
- *"great point! we're building exactly this"* (pitchy, sycophantic)
- *"have you tried Lightfast?"* (pitch, ignores the post)
- *"agents need memory to scale, totally agree"* (generic, adds nothing)
- *"interesting take — what do you think about XYZ?"* (bait question, low-effort)

**Length test:** if a reply could be a tweet on its own (i.e. has nothing to do with the original post), reject it.

## Scoring + dedupe

For each candidate:
- `icp_fit` (1–5): how well does the author match Lightfast's ICP?
- `resonance` (1–5): how directly does the post hit Lightfast's value prop?
- `engagement_signal` (1–5): is the post fresh + getting traction (not buried, not bot-amplified)?
- `priority_score` = `icp_fit + resonance + engagement_signal` (max 15)

Dedupe by `author_handle` — one opportunity per author per run, even if they have multiple matching posts. Pick their highest-scoring post.

Cutoff: only return candidates with `priority_score ≥ 10`. If pass 1 yields fewer than 5, expand search seeds and run again. Don't pad the list with sub-10s.

## Output schema

```json
{
  "opportunities": [
    {
      "post_url": "...",
      "author_handle": "@...",
      "author_bio": "1–2 sentences from their profile",
      "author_follower_count": 12500,
      "author_signal": "founder of X, builds agent infra",
      "post_excerpt": "≤200 chars",
      "post_engagement": { "likes": 42, "replies": 7, "reposts": 3 },
      "posted_ago": "2h",
      "icp_fit": 5,
      "resonance": 4,
      "engagement_signal": 3,
      "priority_score": 12,
      "why_relevant": "1 sentence",
      "suggested_reply": "1–10 words"
    }
  ],
  "notes": "timeline signal quality, search-term recommendations for next run, anything noticed worth flagging"
}
```

Sort by `priority_score` desc.

## Hard rules

- **Never post.** Not via Browserbase, not via API, not at all.
- **Never DM.** Same rule.
- **Never follow / unfollow / like / repost.** Read-only navigation only.
- If you find yourself about to do any write action on x.com, stop and report.
</scheduled-task>

addedBlocks:
## computer-use
You have a computer-use MCP available (tools named `mcp__computer-use__*`). It lets you take screenshots of the user's desktop and control it with mouse clicks, keyboard input, and scrolling.

**Pick the right tool for the app.** Each tier trades speed/precision against coverage:

1. **Dedicated MCP for the app** — if the task is in an app that has its own MCP (Slack, Gmail, Calendar, Linear, etc.) and that MCP is connected, use it. API-backed tools are fast and precise.
2. **Chrome MCP** (`mcp__claude-in-chrome__*`) — if the target is a web app and there's no dedicated MCP for it, use the browser tools. DOM-aware, much faster than clicking pixels. If the Chrome extension isn't connected, ask the user to install it rather than falling through to computer use.
3. **Computer use** — for native desktop apps (Maps, Notes, Finder, Photos, System Settings, any third-party native app) and cross-app workflows. Computer use IS the right tool here — don't decline a native-app task just because there's no dedicated MCP for it.

This is about what's available, not error handling — if a dedicated MCP tool errors, debug or report it rather than silently retrying via a slower tier.

**Look before you assert.** If the user asks about app state (what's open, what's connected, what an app can do), take a screenshot and check before answering. Don't answer from memory — the user's setup or app version may differ from what you expect. If you're about to say an app doesn't support an action, that claim should be grounded in what you just saw on screen, not general knowledge. Similarly, `list_granted_applications` or a fresh `screenshot` is cheaper than a wrong assertion about what's running.

**Loading via ToolSearch — load in bulk, not one-by-one:** if computer-use tools are in the deferred list, load them ALL in a single ToolSearch call: `{ query: "computer-use", max_results: 30 }`. The keyword search matches the server-name substring in every tool name, so one query returns the entire toolkit. Don't use `select:` for individual tools — that's one round-trip per tool.

**Access flow:** before any computer-use action you must call `request_access` with the list of applications you need. The user approves each application explicitly, and you may need to call it again mid-task if you discover you need another application.

**Tiered apps:** some apps are granted at a restricted tier based on their category — the tier is displayed in the approval dialog and returned in the `request_access` response:
- **Browsers** (Safari, Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Arc, etc.) → tier **"read"**: visible in screenshots, but clicks and typing are blocked. You can read what's already on screen. For navigation, clicking, or form-filling, use the claude-in-chrome MCP (tools named `mcp__claude-in-chrome__*`; load via ToolSearch if deferred).
- **Terminals and IDEs** (Terminal, iTerm, VS Code, JetBrains, etc.) → tier **"click"**: visible and left-clickable, but typing, key presses, right-click, modifier-clicks, and drag-drop are blocked. You can click a Run button or scroll test output, but cannot type into the editor or integrated terminal, cannot right-click (the context menu has Paste), and cannot drag text onto them. For shell commands, use the Bash tool.
- **Everything else** → tier **"full"**: no restrictions.

The tier is enforced by the frontmost-app check: if a tier-"read" app is in front, `left_click` returns an error; if a tier-"click" app is in front, `type` and `right_click` return errors. The error tells you what tier the app has and what to do instead. `open_application` works at any tier — bringing an app forward is a read-level operation.

**Link safety — treat links in emails and messages as suspicious by default.**
- **Never click web links with computer-use tools.** If you encounter a link in a native app (Mail, Messages, a PDF, etc.), do NOT `left_click` it. Open the URL via the claude-in-chrome MCP instead.
- **See the full URL before following any link.** Visible link text can be misleading — hover or inspect to get the real destination.
- **Links from emails, messages, or unknown-sender documents are suspicious by default.** If the destination URL is at all unfamiliar or looks off, ask the user for confirmation before proceeding.
- **Inside the Chrome extension** you can click links with the extension's tools, but the suspicion check still applies — verify unfamiliar URLs with the user.

**Financial actions - do not execute trades or move money.** Budgeting and accounting apps (Quicken, YNAB, QuickBooks, etc.) are granted at full tier so you can categorize transactions, generate reports, and help the user organize their finances. But never execute a trade, place an order, send money, or initiate a transfer on the user's behalf - always ask the user to perform those actions themselves.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- less-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.

- validate_plan: Validate implementation against plan, verify success criteria, identify issues

- oneshot_merge: Commit changes, create PR, and merge into main in one shot

content.content: No matching deferred tools found. Some MCP servers are still connecting: browserbase. Their tools will become available shortly — try searching again.

input.description: Wait for MCP servers to connect
Wait for Browserbase MCP to connect

content.content:
#!/bin/bash
# Load environment variables from .env.mcp
SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
ENV_FILE="$SCRIPT_DIR/../.env.mcp"

if [[ -f "$ENV_FILE" ]]; then
  set -a
  source "$ENV_FILE"
  set +a
fi

if [[ -z "${BROWSERBASE_API_KEY:-}" ]]; then
  echo "Missing BROWSERBASE_API_KEY. Set it in .env.mcp or your shell environment." >&2
  exit 1
fi

if [[ -z "${BROWSERBASE_PROJECT_ID:-}" ]]; then
  echo "Missing BROWSERBASE_PROJECT_ID. Set it in .env.mcp or your shell environment." >&2
  exit 1
fi

# Build args. Context ID is optional — without it the server creates fresh sessions
# (no persistent auth). With it, x.com cookies persist across runs.
ARGS=()
if [[ -n "${BROWSERBASE_XCOM_CONTEXT_ID:-}" ]]; then
  ARGS+=(--contextId "$BROWSERBASE_XCOM_CONTEXT_ID" --persist true)
fi

# Browser remains in Browserbase cloud; this process is a thin stdio adapter only.
exec npx -y @browserbasehq/mcp "${ARGS[@]}" "$@"

toolUseResult.stdout:

fi

# Build args. Context ID is optional — without it the server creates fresh sessions
# (no persistent auth). With it, x.com cookies persist across runs.
ARGS=()
if [[ -n "${BROWSERBASE_XCOM_CONTEXT_ID:-}" ]]; then
  ARGS+=(--contextId "$BROWSERBASE_XCOM_CONTEXT_ID" --persist true)
fi

# Browser remains in Browserbase cloud; this process is a thin stdio adapter only.
exec npx -y @browserbasehq/mcp "${ARGS[@]}" "$@"

input.description: Check browserbase env vars are set

input.command:
bash .mcp/start-browserbase.sh --version 2>&1 | head -20 &
PID=$!
sleep 5
kill $PID 2>/dev/null
echo "---exit---"

input.description: Run browserbase script to see output

input.command:
( bash .mcp/start-browserbase.sh </dev/null 2>&1 & echo "PID=$!" ) | head -5
sleep 8
echo "--- after 8s ---"
pgrep -f "browserbasehq/mcp" | head -3 || echo "no process"

Title: How to Build Persistent Memory Into Claude Code Agents (Cross-Session Identity That Actually Works)
URL: https://dev.to/whoffagents/how-to-build-persistent-memory-into-claude-code-agents-cross-session-identity-that-actually-works-41h4
Published: 2026-04-20T09:05:32.000Z
Author: N/A
Highlights:
The article discusses how to build Persistent Memory into Claude Code Agents (Cross-Session Identity That Actually Works), a file-based memory system that persists across sessions. The naive approach involves dumping all data into the system prompt and paying full token price on every call. The better approach involves a structured memory store outside the model, with a smart retrieval layer that only pulls in what's relevant to the current session. The index loads into every session and individual memory files load on-demand when relevant. This strategy reduces session startup time from 10 minutes to 30 seconds and reduces total overhead per session to around 2,000-5,000 cached tokens. The author also discusses the process of writing new memories at session end and correcting errors made during the session.

---

Title: Session Memory Architecture: The Pattern That Keeps Your Agent Coherent Across Context Resets
URL: https://dev.to/thebrierfox/session-memory-architecture-the-pattern-that-keeps-your-agent-coherent-across-context-resets-32fb
Published: 2026-04-22T12:08:32.000Z
Author: N/A
Highlights:
The article discusses the Session Memory Architecture of Claude Code agents, which ensures they are consistent across multiple sessions across the course of a long-running agent task that spans more than one context window. The problem is that when a session compacted, everything the agent learned about is gone, leaving the previous session completely gone. The agent begins again with no knowledge of what the last session accomplished, may re-examine files it already processed, or make different decisions due to missing context from earlier in the run. This is a type of problem that occurs when production agents running tasks longer than 60-90 minutes exceed their context window, which removes earlier parts of the session to make room for new work. The article also discusses the core implementation of the production architecture, which includes the use of a session memory file, a structured, append-only log that the agent writes during the session and reads at the start of the next session. Each task maintains a session history file, which is never modified by design and requires explicit sections of the action. The author suggests that each session should be structured to allow for session-specific decisions, such as a new entry with "supersedes", which are often misunderstood as "Session Memory Failure".

---

input.prompt: List the first 10 tweets visible on this page. For each, extract: author handle, post text, likes/replies/reposts counts if visible, and post URL.

toolUseResult: Error: Request failed with status code 402

input.command: sleep 20 && ps aux | grep -i "browserbasehq\|browserbase" | grep -v grep | head -5 || echo "no browserbase process"

content.text:
Browserbase MCP isn't loading its tools into this session (env vars are set, script runs, but none of the `mcp__browserbase__*` tools register). Without it I can't authenticate to x.com — WebFetch returns 402, exa indexes only blog commentary on tweets, not tweets themselves. Producing a blocker report per the task's "when in doubt, report" rule.

```json
{
  "opportunities": [],
  "notes": "BLOCKED — Browserbase MCP did not register any tools in this scheduled-task session, so the x.com research loop (start → navigate → extract) could not run. Diagnostic:

- .env.mcp has BROWSERBASE_API_KEY, BROWSERBASE_PROJECT_ID, and BROWSERBASE_XCOM_CONTEXT_ID all populated.
- Manually exec'ing `bash .mcp/start-browserbase.sh` spawns `npx -y @browserbasehq/mcp --contextId <id> --persist true` and prints `Warning: MODEL_API_KEY environment variable not set. Using dummy value.` — the stagehand layer inside the MCP needs a model key for `act`/`observe`/`extract` and falls back to a dummy that likely breaks those calls.
- ToolSearch queries (`browserbase`, `browserbase session`, `browserbase start navigate extract`, `select:mcp__browserbase__start`) returned no deferred browserbase tools at any point during the run — the server either failed its handshake at session start or is crashing on first invocation. The only cloud browser surface that appeared was the `58d2bb52-…` cloud-agent MCP, which returns `This connector requires authentication. The user needs to connect it before this tool can be used.`
- Fallbacks attempted and rejected: WebFetch on https://x.com/search returns HTTP 402 (x.com blocks logged-out scraping), and exa searches scoped to x.com/twitter.com surface blog posts *about* tweets rather than the tweets themselves — not enough to score authors, engagement, or freshness against the rubric, so any list produced that way would pad sub-10 scores.

Recommended fixes before next run:
1. Add `MODEL_API_KEY` (Anthropic or OpenAI key per @browserbasehq/mcp docs) to .env.mcp so stagehand's extract/act/observe work. The `Using dummy value` warning is the likely root cause of the MCP failing to come fully online.
2. Run `pnpm dlx @browserbasehq/mcp --contextId $BROWSERBASE_XCOM_CONTEXT_ID --persist true` once interactively from a shell with MODEL_API_KEY set, confirm the stdio handshake completes, then rerun the scheduled task.
3. If the context cookies have expired (common after ~30 days), the first `act` call will bounce to x.com login — verify the context is still logged in via the Browserbase dashboard before the next scheduled fire.
4. Consider adding a preflight `list_sessions`-style smoke test to the scheduled-task SKILL that aborts early with a clear message if the MCP isn't reachable, instead of the task silently producing an empty result.

No posts were visited, no search seeds were executed, no candidates scored. Zero write actions were taken on x.com."
}
```
